name: Hyperkalaemia - Moderate
identifyBy: Tall, narrow, peaked T waves ('tent-shaped') - symmetrical and sharply pointed
T wave amplitude often >6mm in precordial leads
QRS normal width at moderate levels (widening occurs at severe levels)

measurements.qtInterval: Short (early); may prolong with QRS widening

management: Confirm with serum potassium - ECG changes appear at K+ 5.5–6.5 mmol/L (moderate)
IV Calcium gluconate 10mL of 10% over 2–5 min: membrane stabilisation (does NOT lower K+)
Insulin + glucose: 10 units actrapid in 50mL of 50% dextrose IV over 15–30 min (drives K+ into cells)
Salbutamol nebuliser 10–20mg: additional cellular shift (additive to insulin)
Consider sodium bicarbonate if acidotic (1–2 mmol/kg IV)
Definitive: dialysis, cation exchange resins (e.g. patiromer) - hospital definitive care
Identify and treat cause: renal failure, ACEi/ARB, spironolactone, rhabdomyolysis, Addison's disease
pearls: Progressive ECG changes: tall T → flat P → wide QRS → sine wave → VF/asystole
Calcium gluconate stabilises cardiac membranes within minutes - give FIRST if haemodynamically compromised
Pseudohyperkalaemia: haemolysed sample gives falsely elevated K+ - always recheck clinical correlation
Severe hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 mmol/L with wide QRS): treat as emergency regardless of symptoms
Hyperkalaemia + digoxin toxicity: DO NOT give calcium (may precipitate stone heart syndrome - IV calcium contraindicated in digoxin toxicity)
differentials: Severe hyperkalaemia (ECG is a spectrum - more changes = higher K+)
Normal high T waves (in young patients, V2–V4 precordial, asymmetric)
STEMI hyperacute T waves (asymmetric, with ST elevation)
De Winter T waves (upsloping ST depression with tall T - LAD equivalent)
